note: Text-only index of historically attested erotic artworks, compiled via a two-pass research process (independent sourcing, then adversarial fact-check against museum catalogs, auction records, and cited scholarship). Confidence: high = independently confirmed against a primary source; medium = core facts confirmed, some detail unverified; low = existence plausible but key facts unconfirmed. Corrections found during verification are preserved in the note field rather than smoothed over. depicts[] links works to catalog positions ONLY where the source description names the arrangement. All prose original; work titles, dates, holdings, and accession numbers are facts of the public record.

traditions.title: Shunga — Edo-period ukiyo-e, 1603–1868
traditions.overview: A large sub-genre of ukiyo-e spanning single-sheet prints, printed albums, and hand-painted scrolls by nearly every major master. Recurring conventions: enlarged genitalia against heavily robed figures, with position often signaled through drapery; built-in voyeuristic framing (screens, peering figures); a register running from tender to comic-domestic; and a real but overstated "sex-education" function per current scholarship (Clark, Gerstle, Screech).

traditions.title: Ming–Qing Erotic Albums — "Spring Pictures," c. 1590–1900
traditions.overview: Two parallel modes: unique hand-painted silk albums (court or literati commissions, circulated as connoisseurial objects) and commercial polychrome woodblock albums. The standard modern source is James Cahill's Chinese Erotic Painting; van Gulik's 1951 publications remain influential but are now contested on authenticity grounds.

traditions.title: Khajuraho, Konark & the Kama Sutra Manuscript Tradition
traditions.overview: Temple sculpture (c. 950–1250 CE, Chandela and Eastern Ganga patronage) and manuscript painting diverge sharply in register: architectural maithuna is concentrated at structurally significant thresholds, while manuscript painting serves courtly, dynastic, and erotological functions centuries later.

traditions.title: Attic Vase Painting — Symposium & Erotic Scenes
traditions.overview: Explicit imagery concentrates in a narrow window (c. 520–460 BC) and a narrow object class: the kylix used at the andrōn symposium. Placement within the vessel is meaningful — interior tondo scenes are revealed only as wine is drunk, while restrained courtship imagery occupies the public exterior.

traditions.title: Pompeii, Herculaneum & the Silver-and-Ceramic Repertoire
traditions.overview: Roman explicit imagery spans elite silver (the Warren Cup), public bathhouse fresco, brothel decoration, and mass-produced tableware — all drawing on a shared repertoire that later 18th–20th century reception history suppressed as the Naples "Secret Cabinet" and the British Museum's "Secretum."

traditions.title: Tomb Painting, Necropolis of Monterozzi, Tarquinia
traditions.overview: Explicit imagery is rare and tightly circumscribed: of roughly 200 known painted chamber tombs at Monterozzi, only two contain erotic scenes — the Tomb of the Bulls and the Tomb of the Whipping. This scarcity, against the relative commonness of erotic imagery on contemporary Attic pottery, is itself a documented contrast.

traditions.title: Moche Culture, Peru — Huaco Erótico Stirrup-Spout Ceramics
traditions.overview: c. 100–800 CE, north-coast Peru. At least 500 sexually-themed vessels survive, cataloged in depth by the Museo Larco. Anal and oral acts substantially outnumber vaginal depiction in the surviving corpus — read by Mary Weismantel as evidence of an Andean "expanded" concept of reproduction, tied more to ritual continuity between living and dead than to procreation itself.

traditions.title: Colima, Nayarit & Jalisco Shaft-Tomb Ceramics
traditions.overview: c. 300 BCE–500/600 CE. The dominant genre is the static "joined couple" — companionate, non-coital pairs encoding marital bond and lineage rather than sexual position. Explicit content, where it appears, skews toward single-figure autoeroticism rather than staged intercourse.

traditions.title: Qajar Painting & Lacquerware; Ottoman Bahname Manuscripts
traditions.overview: 18th–19th century courtly production, largely known today through auction-house cataloguing (Sotheby's, Bonhams) rather than museum permanent collections. Erotic subject matter is documented as an accepted, non-taboo genre among leading Ottoman court painters — a marked contrast with the contemporary Western reception of equivalent material.

traditions.title: I Modi & Renaissance Erotic Engraving, 1524–1600
traditions.overview: A censorship case study as much as an art-historical one. I Modi's undisguised, contemporary (not mythological) figures denied it the classicizing alibi that protected comparable genres — its near-total destruction by papal order is read as a direct index of how transgressive that choice was judged.

traditions.title: 19th–20th Century Erotic Art & Early Erotic Photography
traditions.overview: France, Belgium, and Austria. The camera arrives mid-corpus: the same position repertoire long rendered in print and paint reappears in daguerreotype and gelatin-silver photography, now entangled with real obscenity prosecutions that used pose and framing, not explicitness alone, as the legal test.

traditions.title: Papua New Guinea, Māori Aotearoa & Aboriginal Australia
traditions.overview: Explicit genital imagery here is overwhelmingly instrumental — fertility, genealogy, magical power — rather than narrative-erotic, produced by named ritual specialists for defined audiences. Solitary genital display substitutes for the dyadic coital scene that dominates the Eurasian and American traditions above.

traditions.title: Dogon, Luba & Yoruba Fertility and Generative Sculpture
traditions.overview: Like Oceania, this corpus is dominated by paired-figure symbolism rather than depicted intercourse — nudity here overwhelmingly marks ancestral, cosmogonic, or social status rather than eroticism. No confirmed depiction of an enacted sexual position was located across any of the peoples searched.

works.name: Hokusai, "Tako to Ama" ("The Dream of the Fisherman's Wife")
works.medium: Woodblock album illustration, from Kinoe no Komatsu
works.description: Non-penetrative interspecies contact: an ama diver reclines entwined with a large octopus, its mantle at her genitals, a second, smaller octopus at her mouth.
works.significance: Read by Goncourt as assault when viewed out of context; scholar Danielle Talerico's reinterpretation, situating the image within the album's inscribed dialogue, established the "mutual pleasure" reading now standard in scholarship.

works.name: Utamaro, Utamakura ("Poem of the Pillow")
works.description: Print 10: a couple making love in a teahouse's upper room, a kyōka poem inscribed on a fan. Print 12: a rare depiction of two Europeans having sex.
works.significance: Widely regarded as a high point of Utamaro's erotic output; absorbs Nagasaki-derived "Dutch" imagery as an erotic novelty.

works.name: Torii Kiyonaga, Sode no Maki ("Handscroll for the Sleeve")
works.medium: Hashira-e ("pillar print"), 12 designs mounted as a ~9 m scroll
works.description: The narrow pillar-print format compresses compositions toward standing and elevated-leg embraces.
works.significance: Explicit content in a normally non-erotic bijin-ga format, designed to be carried furtively inside a kimono sleeve.

works.name: Moronobu, "Two Lovers" & "Courtesan with a Young Man beside a Screen"
works.description: "Two Lovers": a seated man with the woman astride him, before a painted screen. The second work depicts a wakashū (adolescent male) beside a screen.
works.significance: Moronobu founded ukiyo-e figure printing; the pairing documents nanshoku (male–male) subject matter in his oeuvre.
works.note: Correction — independent sources describe an active woman-astride pose, not the more ambiguous drapery-obscured embrace originally assumed.

works.name: Miyagawa Chōshun, Handscroll of Ten Nanshoku Scenes
works.medium: Ink and colors on silk, 27.2 × 340 cm
works.description: Ten scenes of an older man (nenja) and younger men (wakashū) in flamboyant dress — including an abandoned game of go and a hair-dressing scene.
works.significance: Chōshun's school never produced woodblock prints — this is a unique painted, not printed, record of a male–male courtship convention.

works.name: Anonymous, shunga handscroll of intimacy between two women
works.medium: Ink and pigment on paper, 25.8 × 224 cm
works.description: Two figures concealed by robes on a futon; curator Annika Aitken raises the possibility the smaller figure is a wakashū, not a woman, based on an ambiguous hair ornament.
works.significance: A blue bird on a painted screen behind the pair functions as a symbolic witness — a recurring shunga framing device.
works.source_ref: NGV curatorial essay, "Interpreting a Shunga Scroll"

works.name: Koryūsai, plate from Fūryū Jūni Kō
works.description: A father has intercourse with his wife while their son practices calligraphy nearby, in the same pictorial space.
works.significance: Exemplifies the comedic-domestic register: parental sex juxtaposed with an ongoing child's lesson.

works.name: Huaying Jinzhen ("Variegated Battle Arrays of the Flowery Camp")
works.description: A sequential 24-leaf survey of intercourse positions, each image faced by a calligraphic poem leaf.
works.significance: Van Gulik called it, with Fengliu Juechang Tu, the technical peak of Ming color printing.
works.note: Caution — scholar Soren Edgren argues van Gulik's own printing blocks were likely later Japanese recuttings, not genuine Ming originals — complicating the "authentic peak" claim.

works.name: Fengliu Juechang Tu ("Pictures of the Height of Sophistication")
works.medium: Polychrome woodblock, carved by a Huang-family engraver of Xin'an
works.description: Leaf B1.15: foreplay, the man "still wearing his cap and boots." Leaf H.02: a couple in a garden viewing an open erotic book — a mise-en-abyme of the genre's own circulation.
works.significance: Dress-as-status-marker convention (official garb signals rank or urgency) recurs across the corpus.

works.name: Erotic leaves attrib. Gu Jianlong, from a Jin Ping Mei illustration album
works.medium: Ink and mineral color on silk
works.description: Roughly 33 of ~200 original leaves are explicit; one scene shows a woman's feet "held up" during intercourse while servants wait nearby.
works.significance: A Manchu-court commission illustrating a Han literati novel — evidence of Qing-court engagement with southern erotic culture; the foot-handling detail resonates with foot-binding as a specifically Han practice.

works.name: "Secret Play on a Spring Night," fenben copy after Zhou Fang
works.date: Purported Tang original, recorded 1618; surviving copy stylistically 19th c.
works.description: Emperor Xuanzong and Yang Guifei, "she in a chair, he on a stool," both largely unclothed, with four attendant women physically bracing the encounter.
works.significance: Furniture encodes hierarchy even within an imperial couple; a later copy documenting later assumptions about Tang-era sexuality.

works.name: "Album S," Qiu Ying decorative tradition
works.description: Female–female proximity and embrace scenes (a woman and girl embracing after watching mating cats) alongside conventional heterosexual pairings.
works.significance: Cahill states directly the leaves "leave little doubt that women were intended as the main audience" — complicating a male-gaze-only assumption for the genre.

works.name: Wang Sheng ten-leaf album ("Album A")
works.description: Opens with a scholar and woman beneath a flowering tree; the sequence concludes with one male–male leaf among otherwise heterosexual scenes.
works.significance: Cahill: "the earliest erotic painting by an identifiable artist presently known."

works.name: "Album L," attrib. Xu Mei
works.description: Intercourse obscured behind bed curtains and windows, figures mostly clothed; includes an implied-incest scene and a bestiality-adjacent scene of mating dogs watched by women and children.
works.significance: A restrained, framing-driven mode contrasting with explicit earlier Ming prints.

works.name: Kandariya Mahadeva Temple, erotic frieze
works.description: South wall of the antarala: a central male figure in an inverted, headstand-derived posture, supported by attendants, beside standing/seated mithuna couples.
works.significance: Positioned specifically at the mandapa–garbhagriha structural joint — apotropaic, "weak-point" marking rather than narrative decoration.

works.name: Lakshmana Temple, maithuna niches and plinth frieze
works.description: Wall-niche mithuna couples interspersed with deity figures.
works.significance: Auspicious/protective function, generally consistent with the wider Khajuraho program.
works.note: Caution — the widely-repeated "man with a horse" plinth panel could not be confirmed against the sources checked; Wikipedia and World History Encyclopedia describe the plinth frieze only as a processional scene. Don't cite without independent confirmation.

works.name: Vishvanatha Temple, erotic relief group
works.description: Maithuna figures at the vestibule–sanctum junction, plus acrobatic positions on the north and south walls.
works.significance: The same threshold-placement convention recurs across multiple Chandela temples — a programmed architectural pattern, not one-off decoration.

works.name: Konark Sun Temple, jagamohana exterior mithuna bands
works.description: Couples shown across courtship through coital stages, interspersed with other figural imagery rather than isolated in dedicated erotic bands.
works.significance: The popular claim that the carvings illustrate "every bandha in the Kama Sutra" is an unsourced tertiary claim, not established scholarship — flagged, not repeated as fact.

works.name: "An Amorous Couple, probably Raja Mahendra Pal of Basohli with a Favorite Queen"
works.medium: Gum tempera and gold on paper, Pahari/Basohli school
works.description: Standing pose: the male figure lifts the female figure in embrace on a terrace, with wine, fruit, betel leaf, and discarded garments in the foreground.
works.significance: Fuses ruler portraiture with an erotic posture to assert dynastic virility.

works.name: "Illustration from the Kama Sutra of Kumari and King in Coitus"
works.medium: Opaque watercolor and gold on paper, Nepalese/Newar
works.description: A facing coital depiction between a royal figure and a female figure associated with mother-goddess (Kumari) iconography.
works.significance: Ties ritualized royal union to belief in securing the kingdom's prosperity; embeds real Kathmandu landmarks.

works.name: "Ragini Vilaval" ("Gazing Fondly in the Mirror"), attrib. Natthu
works.holding: San Diego Museum of Art, Binney Collection (1990.783)
works.medium: Opaque watercolor and gold, Bikaner school
works.description: A single woman seated alone on a terrace, gazing at her own reflection after her toilette — a stock Ragamala (musical-mode) conceit.
works.significance: Included as a cautionary example, not an erotic work: long informally cited as Kama Sutra illustration, but verification found no couple and no coital content at all.
works.note: Correction — should be dropped from any "erotic art" list; it is a Ragamala genre portrait.

works.name: Lazzat al-Nisa manuscript tradition
works.holding: Wellcome Collection, State Museum Hyderabad, BnF Paris
works.medium: Illustrated manuscript, opaque watercolor with gilding
works.description: A fourfold female somatic/temperament typology (Padmini, Chitrini/Shankhini, Sankhini, Hastini) paired with recommended coital approaches for each type.
works.significance: Exemplifies Sanskrit-to-Perso-Islamic transmission of erotology; current scholarship treats the typology as a contested site for reading objectification versus empowerment.

works.name: Kylix, Pedieus Painter ("Louvre G13")
works.description: Modest kissing couple in the interior tondo; an explicit multi-figure exterior frieze — a woman bent over a stool penetrated from behind while a separate crouching woman performs fellatio nearby.
works.significance: A paradigmatic Attic "group scene" (Kilmer's "pornotopia"); the kalos inscription "Epilykos kalos" ties it to citizen praise-culture.

works.name: Peithinos Cup
works.description: The exterior carries two courtship-procession friezes, pederastic and heterosexual, using a codified non-penetrative gestural code, not explicit acts.
works.significance: A key type-piece for the standard scholarly reading of Athenian courtship-gesture grammar.

works.name: Kylix, Triptolemos Painter
works.description: Frontal, face-to-face intercourse on a couch, the woman's legs raised.
works.note: Correction — earlier summaries describing this cup as "standing rear-entry anal sex" were conflating it with the separately catalogued Akestorides Painter cup below. Direct inspection of the tondo (via Wikimedia/Wikidata) confirms the frontal pose.

works.name: Kylix, Akestorides Painter
works.holding: National Archaeological Museum, Athens, Vlastos-Serpieris Collection (ΒΣ 541)
works.description: Rear-entry (a tergo) intercourse; a stool bears the youth's folded garment, a knotted staff (baktēria, a citizen-status attribute) bisects the field, faded letters survive from a kalos inscription.
works.significance: The museum's own published catalog situates the piece within symposium culture, where courtesans, not wives, participated in group sympotic sex.
works.source_ref: National Archaeological Museum Athens, "Sex and the Athenian Citizens"

works.name: Eurymedon Vase
works.holding: Museum für Kunst und Gewerbe, Hamburg (1981.173)
works.description: Not intercourse but a staged confrontation: an ithyphallic Greek man reaching toward a figure in Persian archer dress, bent forward. Inscription: "I am Eurymedon, I stand bent forward."
works.significance: Read variously as triumphalist commentary on the Battle of the Eurymedon (Dover, Schauenburg), mock-epic burlesque (Pinney), or comic self-implication of the viewer (Davidson) — a genuine, unresolved scholarly debate.

works.name: Kylix, Brygos Painter
works.description: Symposium imagery — a reclining, singing youth — combined with erotic content; fine-grained tondo details remain unconfirmed beyond general description.
works.significance: Situates explicit content literally within its own sympotic setting, complete with drinking and musical paraphernalia.

works.name: The Warren Cup
works.description: Side A: a bearded man and youth reclining, the youth lowering himself via an overhead strap, a boy watching from a doorway. Side B: a younger man penetrating a smaller adolescent.
works.significance: Read by Clarke as depicting unusual mutual tenderness relative to typical Roman pederastic/dominance imagery.

works.name: Erotic fresco cycle, Suburban Baths apodyterium
works.description: Eight panels (seven sexual scenes, one ithyphallic figure): oral and group scenes, rear-entry, and a woman astride a man with a ferret on her leg.
works.significance: Clarke reads the cycle as comedic and mnemonic, tied to numbered locker tokens (spintriae) so bathers could find their clothes shelf.

works.name: Lupanar wall paintings
works.date: Before 79 CE (one dating: 72 CE, via a coin impression)
works.description: Woman-on-top, missionary, rear-entry, a 69 position, and anal sex, plus a Priapus panel — placed deliberately above eye level to create symbolic distance from the viewer.
works.significance: Levin-Richardson and others dispute that the panels functioned as a room-by-room "menu" of services.

works.name: Erotic fresco, House of the Centenary, Room 43
works.description: Two scenes: standard woman-superior, and the rarer "reverse upright Venus" (woman astride, facing away).
works.note: Correction — the claim that the fresco was relocated to Naples could not be confirmed and is likely wrong. The attribution of the "sex club" vs. "ordinary bedroom" readings to specific scholars may also be reversed relative to some summaries — verify against Clarke's primary text before citing either interpretation.

works.name: Pan and the Goat
works.date: Roman, 1st c. BCE–1st c. CE, excavated mid-18th c.
works.description: Pan crouched behind a she-goat in rear-entry copulation, the goat's head turned back toward him.
works.significance: Emblematic of the Secret Cabinet's suppression history and of mythological framing licensing transgressive content.

works.name: Spintriae — numbered erotic tokens
works.date: Two series, c. 22–37 CE and c. 30–79 CE
works.description: Numeral obverse (I–XVI), erotic vignette reverse depicting both male–female and male–male acts.
works.significance: Contested function — brothel token, gaming piece, or (the strongest current theory) bath-locker token, paralleling the Suburban Baths' own numbering system.

works.name: Arretine ware bowl-mould, workshop of M. Perennius Tigranus
works.holding: British Museum (mould); Met Museum (related glass fragment)
works.description: An intimate banquet/embrace scene, non-explicit as preserved.
works.significance: Shows the erotic repertoire of elite silver — including, stylistically, the Warren Cup — also circulating in mass-produced ceramic and glass tableware.

works.name: Tomba dei Tori — male-couple scene
works.description: Two men in rear-entry intercourse above a doorway; a bearded, man-headed, phallic bull charges from the left.
works.significance: Four competing scholarly readings survive side by side: mythological (Achelous, per Oleson), purely apotropaic (Holloway), comic/parodic (Whitehead), and funerary "life-affirming" (Steingräber) — no consensus, and the disagreement is itself the interesting fact.

works.name: Tomba dei Tori — heterosexual-triad scene
works.description: A kneeling man, a woman on his back, and a second man engaging her from behind; a calm, recumbent bull nearby — contrasting the charging bull beside the male couple.
works.significance: Part of the same apotropaic/life-affirming program as the adjacent scene, though the logic of the calm-vs-charging bull contrast remains debated.

works.name: Tomba della Fustigazione — woman between two men
works.description: Two men, "likely having intercourse" with a woman standing between them, separated by a painted false door from the flagellation scene on the same wall.
works.significance: Komos/Dionysiac context on the same wall — dancers, musicians, a nude boxer — supports a ritual reading over a purely genre one.

works.name: Tomba della Fustigazione — flagellation scene
works.description: A bearded man strikes the bending woman with his open hand while a separate, beardless man behind her holds the raised whip that names the tomb.
works.significance: Rarity of an erotic-flagellation motif in the wider corpus; read as ritualistic, part of an apotropaic program meant to "keep demons from the tomb."
works.note: Correction — earlier summaries attributing all striking action to one man (the whip-holder alone) miss that both flanking men are simultaneously erotic and disciplinary actors.

works.name: Pre-Penetration Scene, ML004214
works.description: A woman reclines supine, legs raised and supported, immediately before rear-entry anal penetration.
works.significance: The man's cap marks a Mochica priest; Weismantel reads such scenes as depicting ritually significant, high-status figures.

works.name: Fellatio Scene with Priest Figure, ML004282
works.description: A kneeling woman performs fellatio on a man wearing a priest's cap.
works.significance: Curatorial text ties the scene to the man's nearness to death — his continued capacity "to keep emitting semen" as he approaches the underworld.
works.note: Correction — the museum classifies this specifically as fellatio, not generic "genital contact" as sometimes summarized.

works.name: Fellatio Vessel with Disproportionate Female Figure, ML004284
works.description: A kneeling woman performs fellatio on a warrior-dressed man; she is rendered at markedly larger scale.
works.significance: Curatorial text reads the scale disparity as an ancestral, earth-linked female authority receiving seed from a younger warrior — propitiatory, not narrative-erotic.

works.name: Masturbation of the Skeletal Ancestor, ML004341
works.description: A living woman manually stimulates a deceased, cadaverous male figure; stepped motifs signify the underworld (Uku Pacha).
works.significance: A central case study in Weismantel's argument that these scenes propitiate the dead through the "emission of real or symbolic semen," maintaining ritual continuity rather than depicting procreation.

works.name: Mythological Union of Ai Apaec and the Earth, ML004359
works.description: A fanged supernatural figure (Ai Apaec) copulates with a human woman; an ulluchu tree grows from her genital region, monkeys harvesting its fruit.
works.significance: A sacred-register scene encoding agricultural regeneration, distinct from the funerary/ancestor scenes above.

works.name: Reclining Woman with Genital Vessel-Orifice, ML004426 / ML004200
works.description: A solitary reclining woman whose vulva serves as the vessel's liquid intake/pour spout.
works.significance: Curatorial text frames this as symbolizing fertility, childbirth, and ritual consumption together — cited in Weismantel's argument for treating the pots as active bodies, not passive images.

works.name: The "Siamese Twins" Vessel
works.holding: Reported at Museo Larco; catalog number unconfirmed
works.description: Two adult men shown seated and embracing in mirrored posture.
works.significance: For much of the 20th century classified under a typology of pathology ("conjoined twins"); Wołoszyn & Piwowar (2015, American Anthropologist 117(2)) reanalyze it as a rare candidate for depicted male–male affection.

works.name: Joined Couple, Proctor Stafford Collection
works.description: Two seated figures joined side by side, each holding a vessel or object — a companionate pair, not a coital scene.
works.significance: Exemplifies the dominant West Mexican "joined couple"/ancestor-pair type, read as marital or lineage bonding.

works.name: "Pair of Figure Vessels," attrib. El Chanal
works.date: 1200–1500 CE (Postclassic), per the Met's own record
works.description: A male and female figure vessel presented as a matched pair.
works.note: Correction — this object dates 700–1,800 years after the shaft-tomb tradition ended and is culture-tagged "Nayarit," not "Colima," by the Met itself. It should not be cited as a shaft-tomb example — a genuine misattribution caught in verification, not a hedge.

works.name: Pair of Figures
works.holding: Metropolitan Museum of Art (1978.412.156); Iztlán del Río findspot
works.description: Paired standing/seated male and female figures — companionate, not coital.
works.significance: A Timeline of Art History highlight object exemplifying the ancestor/origin-couple genre.

works.name: Seated Couple
works.description: A seated man and woman joined at the legs, facing each other; she holds a drinking bowl for him, his arm draped over her shoulder.
works.significance: The museum's own text reads this as a possible marriage-ceremony image — companionate bonding, not intercourse, is the genre's dominant mode.

works.name: "Man Masturbating and Touching His Anus" (catalog piece 180)
works.description: A single male figure in an autoerotic gesture.
works.significance: Cited by exhibition scholarship as evidence of "socially acceptable" pleasures including anal stimulation, read by the reviewer as consistent with a homosexual orientation, alongside sparser Maya evidence for pre-Hispanic sexual diversity.

works.name: "Men Pleasuring Themselves" (pieces 181–182)
works.description: Two further single-figure male masturbation pieces.
works.significance: Discussed under the exhibition's "phallic cult" module, linking the phallus to the coa (digging stick) that "penetrates and fertilizes the earth" — contrasted with the colonial Catholic framing of masturbation as "wasted seed."

works.name: Colima Scene, "A Man with Five Women"
works.holding: Cited via archaeologist Ángeles Olay Barrientos
works.description: One central male figure with five female figures — a marital/domestic grouping, not a coital position.
works.significance: Cited as material evidence of polygyny, explicitly contrasted with ethnographically documented polyandry in the Isthmus of Tehuantepec — used to argue against treating any single marital structure as "natural."

works.name: Affectionate-Couple Pieces (111, 114, 116, 117)
works.description: Seated or standing man-and-woman pairs in gestures of affection rather than genital contact; piece 116 was reproduced on the exhibition catalog cover.
works.significance: Gender-archaeology scholarship (Rodríguez-Shadow) treats this material as unusual within Mesoamerica for depicting tenderness rather than pure eroticism — it drew collectors like Diego Rivera specifically for that quality.

works.name: Untitled miniature, "lady reclining against a cushion," attrib. 'Abdullah Bukhari
works.holding: Sold Sotheby's, 2012, lot 224; now private collection
works.medium: Gouache and gold on paper, ebru borders
works.description: Semi-reclining, face-to-face courtship pose.
works.significance: Bukhari, with Levni, was among the most celebrated 18th-c. Ottoman court painters; erotic subject matter was "perfectly reasonable" among leading Turkish painters of the era, unlike the contemporary Western taboo.

works.name: Two erotic scenes, attrib. 'Abdullah Bukhari
works.description: Both leaves depict male–male embrace.
works.note: Correction — earlier summaries describing only one leaf as homoerotic are wrong; the Sotheby's catalogue states both leaves in this lot show "two men in an erotic embrace." Of only five documented single-leaf erotic works by Bukhari, at least three are homoerotic.

works.name: Ottoman erotic manuscript — "Tuhfet ül-Mülk" ("The Kings' Gift")
works.description: Heterosexual couples, male–male encounters, lesbian scenes, and hammam bathhouse scenes.
works.significance: Sotheby's called it "one of the most lavish copies of an erotic manual ever produced in Ottoman Turkey"; a recurring turbaned patron figure suggests a personalized commission, within a documented 18th-c. "liberal revival" following 17th-c. conservative backlash.

works.name: Three Qajar erotic miniatures, signed by Lutf'ali
works.description: Two of three leaves show couples/groups in erotic embrace.
works.significance: One leaf carries a dedication — "made for a real dear friend... as a memento" — a documented instance of erotic art functioning as a gift within male homosocial friendship.

works.name: Qajar lacquer mirror-case, male-couple embrace
works.description: Two male figures in intimate embrace on the interior.
works.significance: Evidence of homoerotic imagery integrated into ordinary elite material culture — though sourced only to an informal online post, not an institutional catalog, so treat cautiously.

works.name: Group of Qajar erotic playing cards
works.holding: Sold Bonhams, 2022, lot 85; comparanda in the Khalili Collection
works.description: A man and woman in erotic embrace within a gilt-scrollwork cartouche, alongside non-erotic cards (a horseman, mating lions).
works.significance: Catalogued as likely used "in gentlemanly circles," later repurposed as standalone erotica in at least one comparable set.

works.name: Qajar-period illustrated manuscript, 34 vignettes
works.description: 34 vignettes across 10 folios of couples in intimate embrace, paralleling the Kama Sutra's systematic structure.
works.significance: Scholar Christiane Gruber suggests such manuscripts, "reflecting an outmoded perception of Persian libertinism," were produced for a Euro-American export market — a caution against reading this genre as straightforwardly indigenous production.

works.name: I Modi ("The Ways"/"The Positions"), original engravings
works.date: 1524, destroyed; 2nd ed. with Aretino's sonnets, 1527, destroyed
works.holding: Fragments at the British Museum, Albertina Vienna, Biblioteca Nacional de España
works.medium: Engraving, designed by Giulio Romano, engraved by Marcantonio Raimondi
works.description: Sixteen heterosexual couples, depicted as contemporary rather than mythological figures, across a systematic range of positions; Position 15 shows a woman "leaping astride."
works.significance: No complete original survives anywhere. Talvacchia argues the compositions drew on ancient Roman spintriae; Aretino's sonnets give the women explicit voice, read as casting them as sexual initiators — though this reflects male-authored fantasy, not a literal record of women's autonomy.

works.name: I Modi, later woodcut booklet copy ("Sedici Modi")
works.description: Approximately fifteen of the original sixteen positions survive.
works.significance: The fullest surviving, if degraded, visual witness to the destroyed original cycle — the primary evidence scholars use to reconstruct what the 1524/1527 engravings actually showed.

works.name: The Lovers (Due Amanti), Giulio Romano
works.medium: Oil on panel, transferred to canvas 1834
works.description: A face-to-face reclining embrace, genitalia not fully exposed, witnessed by an older clothed woman at bedside; the bed is decorated with erotic bas-reliefs of satyrs copulating with a goat and a woman.
works.significance: A unique painted panel for private elite (Gonzaga court) patronage rather than public circulation — kept off display until 1920.

works.name: Loves of the Gods, Jacopo Caraglio after Perino del Vaga and Rosso Fiorentino
works.description: Mythological couplings of gods and nymphs in reclining embraces within landscape settings, using "slung-leg" conventions while avoiding full anatomical explicitness.
works.significance: Mythological framing let this series escape the destruction visited on I Modi — one dealer reportedly bought 250 sets of French copies in 1550, evidencing wide survival and circulation.

works.name: Lascivie, Agostino Carracci ("A Satyr Approaching a Sleeping Nymph")
works.description: A pre-coital approach, not penetration: a satyr creeping toward a sleeping nymph, finger to his lips.
works.significance: Described by Bury (2001) as "less explicitly sexual" than earlier series because its actors are mythological/biblical — staging asymmetrical gender dynamics for a voyeuristic viewer.

works.name: De dissectione partium corporis humani, Charles Estienne
works.holding: University of Pittsburgh Falk Library and other rare-book collections
works.description: Erotic woodblocks originally cut for Caraglio's Loves of the Gods were physically overlaid with anatomical detail and reused as medical illustrations.
works.significance: The clearest documented instance of literal pedagogical repurposing in the whole corpus — the same posed bodies carrying erotic charge in one context and clinical instruction in another.

works.name: "Musée des familles" erotic lithograph suite, attrib. Achille Devéria
works.description: A narrative arc — Childhood, "The Bride," "A Good Position," "All Places Are Good"... through "The Discovery," "The Official Report" — mimicking, while mocking, the marriage-manual genre.
works.significance: Devéria's dual identity — respected Salon lithographer and, from 1849, director of the BnF print room, alongside semi-anonymous author of explicit suites — illustrates the era's split between public academic practice and private libertine production.

works.name: Forty Erotic Watercolors, attrib. Peter Fendi
works.date: Biedermeier Vienna, early 19th c.; posthumous 1910 facsimile (600 copies)
works.description: Six plates show "straightforward" heterosexual scenes; seven others show "humorous and outright acrobatic" scenes, some incorporating musical instruments.
works.significance: Fendi's public oeuvre was decorous Biedermeier genre painting — the erotic watercolors exemplify class-stratified, dual-track production for a closed private commission.

works.name: Erotic daguerreotypes, Félix-Jacques Moulin
works.description: "[Two Standing Female Nudes]": a standing, frontal paired female nude pose.
works.significance: Moulin was imprisoned a month in 1851; the court held the works "so obscene that even to pronounce the titles... would violate public morality" — a documented case where pose and framing, not explicitness alone, marked the legal line of obscenity.

works.name: Erotic stereo-daguerreotypes, Auguste Belloc
works.holding: Bibliothèque nationale de France, Collection de l'Enfer
works.description: Predominantly solo female nude studies, seated or reclining; 24 stereo views survive of an original ~5,000 seized.
works.significance: A case study historians use to trace how Second Empire policing of erotic photography shaped both commercial nude-photography style and the formation of restricted state archives like the Enfer.

works.name: "Woman Sitting on a Man to Engage in Coitus," anonymous
works.description: Woman-superior, seated-astride, photographed in a studio setting.
works.significance: Exhibited in the Kinsey Institute's 2015 "Hold That Pose" show (curated by Catherine Johnson-Roehr) as early photographic evidence of a position repertoire long depicted in painted and printed erotica, now captured by the camera.

works.name: Pornokratès, Félicien Rops
works.medium: Gouache, watercolor, and pastel on paper
works.description: Allegorical, not literal coitus: a nearly nude, blindfolded woman leads a pig on a leash across a frieze of despondent grey male figures personifying the arts.
works.significance: Stages a femme-fatale inversion of gender hierarchy; provoked scandal at the 1886 Cercle des XX exhibition.

works.name: Franz von Bayros, Erzählungen am Toilettentische
works.description: Dealer-characterized content: lesbian bondage, group activity, sadomasochistic scenarios — not independently confirmed in position-level detail.
works.significance: Bayros, working under the pseudonym "Choisy Le Conin," was forced into exile from Munich over this portfolio in the real 1911 "Affäre Semerau" censorship case.

works.name: Gable Support Figure, Iatmul
works.holding: Museums Victoria, Melbourne (187234); Middle Sepik River, PNG
works.description: A single, frontal female figure, legs splayed, genital region exposed toward the viewer — solitary display, not a two-person scene; a totemic lizard carved on the reverse.
works.significance: The museum's own text states men passing beneath it are reminded "of their coming into being, their birth" — a cosmogonic function tied to a men's ceremonial house.

works.name: Painted war shield (vayola), Trobriand/Milne Bay type
works.description: Not a scene at all: a stylized, schematic vulva emblem at the shield's center, flanked by paired serpentine "red snake" bands.
works.significance: Trobriand cosmology locates magical power in the belly; the vulva motif is the channel through which a war magician projects power, paired with a feared flying witch-spirit, Mulukwausi.

works.name: Door lintel (pare), Rongowhakaata style
works.holding: British Museum, London (Oc1854,1229.89); Poverty Bay, NZ
works.description: A central frontal female figure, legs apart, in a generative/parturition posture, genital area carved as a secondary masked face — not a two-person coital scene.
works.significance: Read alternately as Papatūānuku giving birth to the principal gods, or Hinenuitepō defeating Māui; marks the threshold between tapu interior and noa exterior.

works.name: Amo (support figure), pātaka Te Puawai-o-Te-Arawa, by Wero Tāroi
works.holding: Auckland War Memorial Museum; originally Maketū, Bay of Plenty
works.description: An interlocked, embracing couple carved on the storehouse's support posts.
works.significance: Documented within Ngāhuia Te Awekōtuku's scholarship on Māori carving and sexuality — a fertility/abundance statement for a communal food-storage structure.

works.name: Yirawala, "Mimihs Love Making — Wubarr Ceremony"
works.holding: Sold via Sotheby's, 2009; painted at Minjilang, W. Arnhem Land
works.description: Ancestral Mimi spirit figures in coitus, part of a sequential narrative tied to the Wubarr ceremony.
works.significance: Yirawala, popularly called the "Picasso of Arnhem Land," is among the most documented Aboriginal bark painters to render ceremonial sexual narrative explicitly.

works.name: Bark painting, paired Kunabibi/Wubar figures
works.holding: British Museum (E_Oc1973-Q-17); W. Arnhem Land, collected by R. and C. Berndt
works.description: Paired male/female figures tied to Kunabibi and Wubar ceremonial narratives, catalogued as a "love magic" painting.
works.significance: Connects to the Berndts' 1951 monograph Sexual Behaviour in Western Arnhem Land, a foundational, if now dated, ethnographic study of the region.

works.name: Door Lock: Couple
works.description: A male and female figure carved together on a functional granary-door lock, in paired contact rather than an enacted act.
works.significance: Adapted from a locking mechanism introduced via trans-Saharan Islamic trade, converted into a carrier of ancestral-couple imagery tied to household fertility and lineage.

works.name: Figure: Seated Couple
works.description: Nude male and female figures seated side by side in mutual contact — a static, non-coital posture typical of the genre.
works.significance: Read within Griaule-derived Dogon cosmology as the mythic first ancestors, modeling marital complementarity; nudity marks ancestral status, not eroticism.

works.name: Door Lock (Dogon)
works.description: Two figures in paired arrangement on the lock face, following the same genre convention as the Met example above.
works.significance: Part of a cross-institutional corpus (also MFA Houston, Dallas Museum of Art) showing how a functional, Islamic-derived lock became a standardized site for ancestral-couple imagery.

works.name: Luba Shankadi "Seated Couple"
works.holding: Private collection, France; offered Bonhams LA, 2016 (withdrawn)
works.description: A kneeling, embracing couple; the man holds a staff of authority, both kneel with arms crossed behind their backs — non-penetrative, dynastic embrace.
works.significance: The auction house's own note calls the paired format "unique within the corpus of Shankadi sculpture," which otherwise favors solitary caryatid and staff figures — read as embodying political alliance and matrilineal succession.

works.name: Luba prestige (caryatid) stool, attrib. Ngongo ya Chintu ("the Buli Master")
works.holding: Comparanda at the Met, MFA Boston, Dallas Museum of Art
works.description: Not a coital scene: a single kneeling female figure physically supports the stool's seat.
works.significance: Mary Nooter Roberts's scholarship reads the kneeling support-figure as a statement of the female body as a vessel supporting divine kingship and matrilineal succession, not a narrow fertility emblem.

works.name: Edan Ogboni, paired staff figures
works.description: A male and female figure, cast separately, standing upright side by side, joined by a chain — not embracing, not coital; even the female figure is conventionally given a beard.
works.significance: Insignia of the Ogboni society of elders, representing complementary aspects of the earth deity (Ilè/Oduduwa) — consistent with Okediji's argument that nudity in Yoruba sculpture more often marks social/economic status than eroticism.

works.name: Baule sexual-position sculpture
works.description: No specific, named Baule work depicting an identifiable sexual position was located.
works.significance: Reported as an honest gap rather than papered over: Baule sculpture (blolo bian/bla spirit-spouse figures) is discussed in the literature (Susan Vogel) in terms of restrained, idealized single-figure beauty, not staged sexual narrative.
